g.data-narration: Welcome to E-Waste and Hazardous Properties - understanding the critical environmental challenge of electronic waste.
E-waste refers to discarded electrical and electronic devices like computers, phones, and appliances reaching end-of-life.
g.text: Discarded electrical/electronic devices at end-of-life: computers, mobiles,
TVs, refrigerators. Global generation: ~50 million metric tons/year
Growing at 3-4% annually - fastest growing waste stream!

g.data-narration: Electronic devices contain toxic substances like lead, mercury, and cadmium that pose severe environmental and health risks.
g.text: Contains toxic substances: Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Hexavalent Chromium,
Brominated Flame Retardants. When improperly disposed → soil/water contamination.
Health risks: Neurological damage, kidney failure, cancer, birth defects.

g.data-narration: E-waste is classified into categories: large appliances, small appliances, ICT equipment, consumer electronics, and lighting equipment.
Six primary hazardous substances are found in e-waste: Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Hexavalent Chromium, BFRs, and PVC.
Lead causes neurological damage and kidney failure, especially in children. Mercury is a potent neurotoxin affecting brain development.
g.text: Found in: CRT glass, solder, circuit boards | Causes: Brain damage,
kidney failure, developmental problems in children, hypertension
Found in: Switches, relays, flat-panel displays | Causes: Brain damage,
respiratory disorders, birth defects, Minamata disease
📍 CRTs contain 2-4 kg lead | 📍 Single fluorescent backlight can contain 0.5 kg mercury

g.data-narration: Cadmium accumulates in kidneys and bones. Hexavalent chromium is carcinogenic. BFRs disrupt endocrine systems as persistent pollutants.
g.text: Causes: Kidney damage, bone demineralization, itai-itai disease
Found in: Metal plating, corrosion inhibitors
Causes: Lung cancer, respiratory problems, skin irritation
Causes: Endocrine disruption, cancer, reproductive issues

g.data-narration: Improper e-waste disposal causes groundwater contamination, soil degradation, air pollution, and bioaccumulation through food chains.
g.text: ⚠ Informal recycling in developing countries: primitive methods causing severe pollution

g.data-narration: E-waste exposure causes respiratory diseases, skin disorders, neurological problems, and increased cancer risk in workers and communities.
g.text: Workers in informal recycling: respiratory diseases,
skin disorders, neurological problems, without protection
Near dumping sites: respiratory infections, birth defects,

g.data-narration: E-waste management methods include source reduction, reuse and refurbishment, material recovery through recycling, and energy recovery.
The waste hierarchy follows: Prevention (best) → Reuse → Recycling → Energy Recovery → Safe Disposal (last resort).
g.text: "Reduce, Reuse, Recycle" - Priority order for environmental protection

g.data-narration: India's E-Waste Management Rules 2022 mandate Extended Producer Responsibility, collection targets, and prohibit certain hazardous substances.
g.text: 📋 Rules replace 2016 version | 📋 Transboundary movement requires prior informed consent

g.data-narration: A CRT monitor contains 2-4kg lead, 0.5kg mercury. Proper recycling involves dismantling, CRT processing, and metal recovery.
g.text: ⚠ Improper disposal: Lead/mercury leach into groundwater over 10-15 years
